attributes.description: Alerts when CouchDB returns an elevated rate of 409 (Conflict) responses, indicating heavy write contention on the same documents. Sustained conflict storms suggest application-level retry loops or concurrent writers competing for the same document IDs.
attributes.name: [CouchDB OTel] Conflict storm (elevated 409 responses)

investigation_guide.blob:
## Conflict Storm (Elevated 409 Responses)

### Triage Steps
1. Identify which CouchDB node is producing the 409s — open the CouchDB overview dashboard and filter by the alerting node.
2. Correlate with HTTP request rate (PUT/POST in particular) — conflicts grow with concurrent write volume on the same documents.
3. Check application logs for the documents being updated — are multiple clients writing to the same `_id`?
4. Look for retry storms: clients that retry on 409 without backoff can amplify the problem.

### Remediation
- Implement exponential backoff and jitter in client retry logic.
- Use CouchDB's bulk update endpoint with `all_or_nothing: false` to handle conflicts gracefully.
- Redesign the data model to avoid hot documents — split frequently-updated state across multiple documents.
- For counter-style updates, consider using a separate document per increment and aggregating on read.

### Threshold Tuning
The default of 10 conflicts/sec is a moderate threshold. Some workloads (e.g. CRDT-style replication) may legitimately produce more conflicts; raise the threshold accordingly. For low-write workloads, lower the threshold to detect smaller anomalies.

esqlQuery.esql:
TS metrics-couchdbreceiver.otel-default
// 409 = update conflicts — spikes indicate write contention on same documents
| WHERE attributes.http.status_code == "409"
| STATS conflict_rate = SUM(RATE(couchdb.httpd.responses))
  BY couchdb.node.name
// Tune threshold: 409/s may be normal under load; >10/s often signals contention
| WHERE conflict_rate > 10.0
| SORT conflict_rate DESC
| LIMIT 20
